nodes.name: Presidential Firsts & Records
nodes.description: Presidential firsts, records, and notable distinctions

items.prompt: This president is the only one ever chosen with a unanimous Electoral College vote — achieving it in both 1789 and 1792. He also set the precedent of a two-term limit. Who was he?
items.explanation:
1st President (1789–1797); won the Electoral College unanimously in both elections — 69 votes in 1789, 132 in 1792
No other president has received a unanimous Electoral College vote; even James Monroe in 1820 received one dissenting vote
Set the two-term precedent by voluntarily declining a third term — a tradition honored until FDR broke it in 1940
Commander of the Continental Army during the Revolutionary War (1775–1783)
Presided over the Constitutional Convention (1787) and was unanimously chosen as its president
Face on the $1 bill and the quarter; Mount Rushmore, Washington state, and the capital city all bear his name

items.prompt: This president was the first to live in the White House, moving in on November 1, 1800, while the building was still unfinished. His wife Abigail famously hung laundry in the East Room. Who was he?
items.explanation:
2nd President (1797–1801); moved into the still-unfinished President's House on November 1, 1800
Abigail Adams hung laundry in the unfinished East Room because there was no suitable outdoor space
Also the first Vice President (1789–1797) under George Washington
First president to be voted out of office — lost the 1800 election to Thomas Jefferson
Died July 4, 1826, the 50th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence — the same day as Jefferson
Father of John Quincy Adams (6th President) — one of only two father-son presidential pairs (the other: the Bushes)
items.cardFront: First to live in the White House

items.prompt: At 43 years and 236 days old when inaugurated in 1961, this president was the youngest person ever elected to the office. He was also the first Roman Catholic president. Who was he?
items.explanation:
35th President (1961–1963); inaugurated at age 43 years, 236 days — the youngest person elected president
Note: Theodore Roosevelt was younger (42) when he assumed the presidency after McKinley's assassination, but he was not elected at that age
First Roman Catholic president — his Catholicism was a major campaign issue in 1960
Delivered the famous inaugural line: 'Ask not what your country can do for you — ask what you can do for your country'
Assassinated in Dallas, Texas on November 22, 1963 by Lee Harvey Oswald; the Warren Commission's lone-gunman conclusion remains debated
Established the Peace Corps (1961) and navigated the Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962), the closest the Cold War came to nuclear war

items.prompt: This president became the youngest person ever to hold the office at age 42 after his predecessor was assassinated in 1901. A Rough Rider and Nobel Peace Prize winner, he later ran on a third-party ticket in 1912. Who was he?
items.explanation:
26th President (1901–1909); became president at 42 years, 322 days after McKinley's assassination — youngest ever to hold the office
Distinction from JFK: Roosevelt was the youngest to serve; Kennedy was the youngest elected (at age 43)
Won the Nobel Peace Prize (1906) for mediating the Russo-Japanese War — first American to win a Nobel Prize
Led the Rough Riders at San Juan Hill in the Spanish-American War (1898)
Ran as the Progressive ('Bull Moose') Party candidate in 1912; was shot during a campaign speech but finished it with the bullet still in his chest
Created 150 national forests, 51 federal bird reservations, and 5 national parks — the 'conservation president'

items.prompt: When sworn in for a second, nonconsecutive term in January 2025, this president became the oldest person ever inaugurated at 78 years and 7 months, surpassing the previous record. Who was he?
items.explanation:
45th and 47th President (2017–2021, 2025–present); inaugurated for his second term at age 78 years, 7 months
Surpassed Joe Biden's record (78 years, 61 days at his 2021 inauguration) as the oldest person inaugurated
Second president to serve nonconsecutive terms — the first was Grover Cleveland (22nd and 24th, 1885–1889 and 1893–1897)
First president with no prior political or military experience before his initial 2016 election
First president impeached twice (2019 and 2021) — acquitted by the Senate both times
Former real estate developer and TV personality (The Apprentice, 2004–2015)

items.prompt: Elected in 2008, this president was the first African American to hold the office. A former U.S. Senator from Illinois and constitutional law professor, he won the Nobel Peace Prize in 2009. Who was he?
items.explanation:
44th President (2009–2017); first African American president of the United States
Won the 2008 election over John McCain with 365 electoral votes; reelected in 2012 over Mitt Romney
Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2009 'for his extraordinary efforts to strengthen international diplomacy' — controversial for being awarded so early in his presidency
Former U.S. Senator from Illinois (2005–2008) and constitutional law professor at the University of Chicago
Signed the Affordable Care Act ('Obamacare') in 2010, the most significant expansion of health coverage since Medicare
Born in Honolulu, Hawaii in 1961 — the first president born outside the contiguous 48 states

items.prompt: This president was the first to be assassinated, shot by John Wilkes Booth at Ford's Theatre on April 14, 1865 — just five days after Lee's surrender at Appomattox effectively ended the Civil War. Who was he?
items.explanation:
16th President (1861–1865); first president to be assassinated
Shot by actor John Wilkes Booth at Ford's Theatre in Washington, D.C. on April 14, 1865 (Good Friday); died the next morning
Booth shouted 'Sic semper tyrannis!' ('Thus always to tyrants') after firing; he was killed by soldiers 12 days later in a Virginia barn
The assassination was part of a broader conspiracy — co-conspirators attacked Secretary of State William Seward (who survived)
Preserved the Union through the Civil War and issued the Emancipation Proclamation (1863)
Tallest president at 6'4"; face on the $5 bill and the penny; carved into Mount Rushmore

items.prompt: This president was the second to be assassinated, shot by Charles Guiteau on July 2, 1881, at a Washington train station. He lingered for 11 weeks before dying, largely because doctors probed the wound with unsterile instruments. Who was he?
items.explanation:
20th President (March–September 1881); second president to be assassinated
Shot by Charles Guiteau at the Baltimore and Potomac Railroad Station in Washington, D.C. on July 2, 1881
Guiteau was a disappointed office-seeker who believed he deserved an ambassadorship for supporting Garfield's campaign
Doctors repeatedly probed the wound with unsterile fingers and instruments; modern analysis suggests he would have survived with proper medical care
Alexander Graham Bell attempted to locate the bullet with an electromagnetic device but failed — likely because the metal bed frame interfered
Died September 19, 1881, after 79 days of suffering; his death spurred passage of the Pendleton Civil Service Reform Act (1883)

items.prompt: This president was the third to be assassinated, shot by anarchist Leon Czolgosz at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York, on September 6, 1901. His death elevated Theodore Roosevelt to the presidency. Who was he?
items.explanation:
25th President (1897–1901); third president to be assassinated
Shot twice by anarchist Leon Czolgosz at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, NY on September 6, 1901; died September 14
Czolgosz concealed a pistol under a bandaged hand in a receiving line; he was executed by electric chair in October 1901
Led the U.S. into the Spanish-American War (1898), which resulted in acquisition of Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines
Won the 1896 election on a gold standard platform against William Jennings Bryan and his famous 'Cross of Gold' speech
His death prompted Congress to formally request Secret Service protection for presidents, beginning in 1902

items.prompt: This president is the only one elected to four terms, serving from 1933 until his death in 1945. His unprecedented tenure led to the 22nd Amendment, which limits presidents to two terms. Who was he?
items.explanation:
32nd President (1933–1945); only president elected four times — won in 1932, 1936, 1940, and 1944
Died in office on April 12, 1945, at the 'Little White House' in Warm Springs, Georgia, just months into his fourth term
The 22nd Amendment (ratified 1951) was a direct response to his four terms, limiting future presidents to two
Led the country through the Great Depression (New Deal) and most of World War II
Diagnosed with polio in 1921 at age 39; used a wheelchair but carefully hid his disability from the public
Consistently ranked among the top three presidents alongside Washington and Lincoln in historians' polls
items.cardFront: Only president elected to four terms

items.prompt: This president served only 31 days in office — the shortest presidency in U.S. history. He was the first president to die in office, and his campaign slogan 'Tippecanoe and Tyler Too' referenced an 1811 battle against Tecumseh's forces. Who was he?
items.explanation:
9th President (March 4–April 4, 1841); served only 31 days — the shortest presidency in U.S. history
Delivered the longest inaugural address ever (8,445 words, nearly 2 hours) on a cold, wet March day without a hat or overcoat
Died April 4, 1841, likely from septic shock (long attributed to pneumonia; modern scholars suspect contaminated White House water)
First president to die in office, triggering the first-ever presidential succession crisis — VP John Tyler claimed full presidential powers
Earned the nickname 'Tippecanoe' for his 1811 victory over Tecumseh's forces at the Battle of Tippecanoe in Indiana
Grandfather of Benjamin Harrison (23rd President) — one of two grandfather-grandson presidential pairs

items.prompt: This president is the only one to resign from office, doing so on August 9, 1974, to avoid almost certain impeachment and removal over the Watergate scandal. He had won a 49-state landslide just two years earlier. Who was he?
items.explanation:
37th President (1969–1974); the only president to resign from office
Resigned August 9, 1974, facing impeachment for obstruction of justice, abuse of power, and contempt of Congress in the Watergate scandal
Won a historic 49-state landslide in 1972 — the contrast with his resignation 21 months later is one of the sharpest reversals in American political history
The Watergate break-in (June 17, 1972) at Democratic National Committee headquarters was a minor burglary; the cover-up destroyed his presidency
Pardoned by successor Gerald Ford on September 8, 1974 — preventing any criminal prosecution
Opened diplomatic relations with China (1972) and signed the Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty with the Soviet Union — major Cold War achievements

items.prompt: This president is the only one to serve two nonconsecutive terms (before Trump), holding the 22nd and 24th presidencies. He won the popular vote three times but lost the Electoral College in 1888 to Benjamin Harrison. Who was he?
items.explanation:
22nd and 24th President (1885–1889, 1893–1897); the first president to serve nonconsecutive terms
Won the popular vote in 1884, 1888, and 1892 — but lost the 1888 Electoral College to Benjamin Harrison despite winning the popular vote
First Democrat elected president after the Civil War (1884); only president counted twice in the numbering system
Had a secret surgery to remove a cancerous tumor from his jaw aboard a yacht in 1893 — kept from the public for 24 years
Faced the severe Panic of 1893, one of the worst economic depressions in U.S. history
Donald Trump became the second president to serve nonconsecutive terms when he won the 2024 election (45th and 47th President)

items.prompt: This president was the first to be impeached by the House of Representatives, in 1868. A former tailor from Tennessee who never attended school, he was acquitted by the Senate by a single vote. Who was he?
items.explanation:
17th President (1865–1869); first president impeached by the House of Representatives
Impeached on February 24, 1868, primarily for violating the Tenure of Office Act by removing Secretary of War Edwin Stanton
Acquitted by the Senate on May 16, 1868, falling one vote short of the two-thirds needed for removal (35–19)
Seven Republican senators broke with their party to vote 'not guilty,' at great personal political cost
Self-taught — his wife Eliza taught him to read and write; he worked as a tailor before entering politics
His lenient Reconstruction policies allowed former Confederates back into power, delaying civil rights for decades

items.prompt: This president was the only one who never married. His orphaned niece Harriet Lane served as White House hostess. His failure to address the secession crisis before the Civil War has made him one of the lowest-ranked presidents. Who was he?
items.explanation:
15th President (1857–1861); the only president who never married
His fiancee Ann Coleman died under mysterious circumstances in 1819 — he claimed he never loved again
Orphaned niece Harriet Lane served as White House hostess and became a popular public figure
His close friendship with Alabama Senator William Rufus King (who became VP under Pierce) has been the subject of 175 years of historical speculation
Failed to address the secession crisis as Southern states began leaving the Union after Lincoln's election in 1860
Consistently ranked among the worst presidents in historians' polls — typically last or near-last alongside Andrew Johnson and Franklin Pierce
